title: St. Theodore Stratelates, St. Amon the Monk, St. Cuthbert of Lindisfarne & St. Photina of Rome
narrative:
title": "St. Theodore Stratelates, St. Amon the Monk, St. Cuthbert of Lindisfarne & St. Photina of Rome

narrative": "title\": \"St. Theodore Stratelates, St. Amon the Monk, St. Cuthbert of Lindisfarne & St. Photina of Rome

narrative\": \"St. Theodore Stratelates the Great Martyr, St. Amon of Egypt the Monk, St. Cuthbert of Lindisfarne, and St. Photina of Rome

On the twentieth of Megabit, the holy Church places before the faithful four witnesses whose lives span the geographic and temporal range of the Christian world from the Egyptian desert to the islands of the North Sea, united in the common witness of lives wholly given to God through the particular instruments of their respective callings. Saint Theodore Stratelates — the military general of Heraclea — returns in the Megabit cycle in the specifically Lenten register, where his warrior's vocabulary of combat, vigilance, and the refusal of surrender takes on the interior meaning that the Lenten tradition always finds in the soldier-martyrs. The Lenten fast is a campaign; the temptations of the fast are the enemy's attacks; the virtue of perseverance through Lent is the warrior's virtue transposed into the register of prayer and abstinence. Theodore who refused to yield under torture is the type of the faster who refuses to yield before comfort and appetite.

Saint Amon the Monk is one of the founders of the great semi-anchoritic settlement of Nitria in the Egyptian desert: a married Egyptian who, together with his wife, consecrated their marriage to God in virginity on their wedding night, and who after eighteen years of this unusual shared asceticism separated by mutual consent, his wife founding a women's monastery on one side of the family estate and Amon withdrawing to the desert of Nitria to found the first hermit colony there. The tradition records that Amon needed to cross the canal that ran through his desert to visit Antony, and that he was reluctant to undress to swim across, upon which an angel transported him across fully clothed. This story, charming in its modesty, points to a deeper truth: that the body consecrated to God in long years of discipline becomes, in a sense, sacred — to be treated with the reverence due to a temple rather than merely a vehicle.

Saint Cuthbert of Lindisfarne is this day's figure of northern island holiness: the most beloved saint of the early English church, a monk and bishop of extraordinary pastoral warmth who served the communities of Northumbria with tireless energy, travelling the most remote dales and islands of his diocese on foot and on horseback, baptising, teaching, and healing. After years of episcopal service he withdrew again to the island of Farne to live as a hermit, surrounded by the grey North Sea and the calling of the seabirds that he had always had a particular tenderness for, and died there in the posture of prayer. His relics, carried by his community through decades of Viking raids, eventually came to rest at Durham, where they remain a place of pilgrimage to this day.

Saint Photina of Rome is the companion figure to the Samaritan Photina commemorated on the fifth day: while the Samaritan woman received Christ's revelation beside a well in Palestine, this Photina — possibly identified with her in some traditions, possibly a separate figure — is honoured as a martyr of Rome who was executed during the Neronian persecution alongside her companions. The tradition that unites the two Photinas in a single story of evangelical mission beginning at Jacob's well and ending in the Tiber is the tradition's way of showing that the living water Christ promised does not stay in one place but flows, through the lives of those who receive it, from the wells of Samaria to the streets of Rome to the islands of Britain to the deserts of Egypt. The promise was not given for one person or one place, and the Lenten community drinking from that promise is drinking from the same source. Amen.
